behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher recursion and OS event delivery for nested paths. A unit/integration fake can inject a nested path event, but that can miss real failures where the watcher is not actually subscribed recursively or misses subdirectory events on the host OS. Rejection gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text is about actual placement in subdirectories under a watched root, which depends on real watcher+OS behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a new subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that file within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Core risk is module handoff/state behavior (request intake -> dedup store/window config -> pipeline trigger), not OS primitives. A realistic in-process test that sends two requests for the same normalized path and controls time can catch real failures. Rejection gate: System is rejected because behavior text does not require real OS/network/subprocess; it is specifically dedup logic over repeated requests within a window.
behaviors.test_description: Wire daemon modules in-process with real dedup state and queue, inject two processing requests for the same path within the configured window, and assert only one pipeline execution occurs; then inject after window expiry and assert processing occurs again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS-level duplicate event emission patterns. Mocking event streams can hide watcher-library/OS-specific burst semantics and timing races. Rejection gate: Integration is rejected by concrete behavior text: "operating system emits multiple filesystem events".
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a single file write pattern known to emit multiple events on the target OS (e.g., write+rename/flush), capture processing invocations, and assert exactly one output artifact for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an inode/path lifecycle condition driven by real filesystem and watcher re-subscription behavior. In-process fakes generally do not reproduce directory deletion/recreation semantics and watcher invalidation. Rejection gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text requires remove/recreate while running, which is an OS-level watcher resilience scenario.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watched directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, place a new file, and assert the daemon processes it without restarting the process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on interaction between real arrival events, filesystem timestamp metadata, and queue scheduling. A pure integration fake can validate sort logic but can miss failures from real timestamp precision/ties and event timing behavior. Rejection gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text anchors order to file creation timestamps at actual arrival time, which is tied to real filesystem metadata behavior.
behaviors.test_description: In a real watched directory, create several files in near-simultaneous fashion, record each file's creation timestamp from filesystem metadata, then assert daemon processing order matches timestamp order (with a defined tie-breaker expectation if equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is a real-time race between OS file events and write completion, often dependent on actual I/O timing/flush behavior. Mocked writes/events frequently hide partial-write pickup bugs. Rejection gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text explicitly requires handling an arrival event during an in-progress write, which is a real filesystem timing condition.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, begin writing a large file in chunks with delays so arrival triggers before completion, verify no processing occurs mid-write, then complete/close the file and assert processing starts afterward and output reflects full content.
